Article.ArticleTitle: Effect of different heat treatments on the physicochemical properties and in vitro digestion of black highland barley flour.

Abstract.AbstractText: Natural black highland barley flour (BHBF) exhibits limited processing performance due to its low gluten protein content; however, thermal treatments can effectively improve its physicochemical properties. In this study, roasting treatment (RT), autoclaving heat treatment (AHT), steam treatment (ST), and microwave treatment (MT) were applied to evaluate their effects on gelatinization behavior, physicochemical characteristics, and in vitro digestibility. All thermal treatments significantly enhanced -SH content and hydration properties, with the elevated water absorption index (WAI) and swelling power (SP). RT increased the storage modulus (G') and loss modulus (G″), indicating improved viscoelastic behavior. Meanwhile, the FTIR results showed that all thermal treatment reduced the amorphous regions of samples. AHT elevated gelatinization temperatures (To: 57.52-67.76 °C; Tp: 62.61-71.04 °C; Tc: 68.53-74.05 °C). Finally, all treatments increased contents of amylose (from 18.67% to 23.05%) and rapidly digestible starch (RDS, from 24.09% to 43.45%). These results provide a scientific basis for developing value-added BHBF-based food products.
